courses.title: Beginning ESL
courses.instructor: Bruno L. Sablan, Mr. David J. Gorman
courses.description: This beginning course provides instruction in English for every day life. Students work on developing basic grammar, listening, speaking, reading and writing skills.

sections.notes: The non-credit equivalent of this course is ESL-850N-3804. CLICK 'NEXT' AT THE BOTTOM OF THIS PAGE TO SEE NON-CREDIT COURSE LISTINGS BEGINNING WITH ESL-808N. courses.title: Reading and Vocab for Beg.
courses.description: This course provides instruction in basic reading and common everyday vocabulary. Students improve their reading skills, expand their vocabulary, and increase their fluency through discussion of reading material. This course supplements material covered in ESL-050.

sections.notes: The non-credit equivalent of this course is ESL-851N-3036 SCROLL DOWN TO THE BOTTOM OF THIS PAGE TO SEE NON-CREDIT COURSE LISTINGS BEGINNING WITH ESL-808N. Section 3026 will meet online with Zoom sessions on Tuesdays, Wednesdays and Thursdays from 6:00 to 7:50pm . Your teacher will contact you with important information prior to the start of the class. For more information, you can contact your instructor at mwalsh@contracosta.edu.

courses.title: Survey of Mass Comm & Media
courses.description: This course provides a survey of mass communication and the interrelationships of media with society, including history, structure and trends in a digital age. The course involves discussion of theories, economics, technology, law and ethics, global media, media literacy, and social issues, including gender and cultural diversity. Emphasizing the social science study of human behavior and institutions, the course examines how individuals and media systems interact within cultural, political, and economic contexts. Students analyze mass communication through core social science methods, exploring evidence, theory, and historical and global perspectives to better understand media's role in shaping society.

sections.notes: Section 3006 is a late-start fully online, asynchronous class. For more information, please contact, Prof. Annie Sciacca (asciacca@contracosta.edu)
Section 3137 is a fully online class with no required meeting times on campus or on Zoom. For more information, please contact instructor Annie Sciacca (asciacca@contracosta.edu).

courses.title: Intro to Research Mthds Psych
courses.description: This course surveys various psychological research methods with an emphasis on research design, experimental procedures, descriptive methods, instrumentation, and the collection, analysis, interpretation, and reporting or research data. Research design and methodology will be examined through a review of research in a variety of the sub-disciplines of psychology. In laboratory sessions students will conduct experimental and non-experimental research in variety of areas of psychology. Actual data collected from research conducted during laboratory sessions will be analyzed with statistical software. Prerequisite: Prerequisite: PSYC-C1000 and STAT-C1000 Advisory: College level reading and writing skills

sections.notes: This is an online class with no required live meeting dates or times (fully asynchronous). Please email ahovasapian@contracosta.edu for more information. The pre-requisties for this course have been renumbered as part of the statewide Common Course Numbering system. PSYC-C1000 was previously named Psych 220. STAT-C1000 was previously named MATH 164. The course content and transferability for both courses remain equivalent

courses.title: African American Humanities
courses.description: This course presents an integrated and historical study of African American culture and African Americans' influence upon American life. Emphasis will be upon philosophy, fine and applied arts, and ways by which they reflect the social, political, and economic realities of a culture.

sections.notes: AFRAM 113/HUM 113 is offered fully online. This is an online class with no required live meeting dates or times (fully asynchronous). You will received a college email before the start of class.

HUMAN-113-3766 is also offered as AFRAM 113-3765. This class is fully online with mandatory live Zoom meetings scheduled at the days and times listed. You will receive a college email prior to the start of class. This class is a part of the AFRAM Studies Program.

courses.title: Intro Analysis/Amer Pol Instit
courses.description: This course examines the U.S. Constitution and the U.S. system of government at the national and state levels, with an emphasis on Latino political participation. The course covers both U.S. and California political processes and highlights the relationships between the federal government and state and local governments. It will also survey the resolution of conflicts and the establishment of cooperative mechanisms under the constitutions of both the state and federal governments. Important events since the American Revolution and contributions of Latinos, women and various ethnic groups will be surveyed. Through a multidisciplinary approach, students will gain a deeper understanding of the challenges, opportunities, and nuances within Latino politics.

courses.title: Library and Information Skills
courses.instructor: Dr. Amy L. Catania, Miss Veronica B. Churchill
courses.description: This is a performance-based course that introduces students to a variety of print and electronic information resources. Students will learn how to find, use and select the most appropriate sources for different information needs, utilize an effective search strategy and evaluate search results. Students will become information competent. This course may be offered in lecture format or online format.

courses.title: World Regional Geography
courses.description: This course provides a study of the world's regions and realms using the framework of cultural geography. This geographic view of the world will be divided between the 12 major geographic realms and the many regions contained within them. Human cultures will be studied spatially, developing an understanding of the interconnectedness of the world.

sections.notes: Section 3072 is a fully online course. All instruction will be offered online. You will receive a college email from your instructor before the class begins. For more information, please contact the instructor at nschillo@contracosta.edu.

courses.title: Introduction to Public Health
courses.instructor: Mr. Nikki A. Ferguson, Beth A. Goehring, Mr. Miguel A. Johnson
courses.description: This course presents an overview of the disciplines of community and public health. Topics include the basic concepts and terminologies of public health; an overview of various public health professions and organizations; the study, prevention and control of diseases in the community; the analysis of the social determinants of health; strategies for eliminating disease, illness, and health disparities among various populations; community organizing and health promotion programming; school health promotion; environmental health and safety; and an overview of the healthcare delivery system in the United States. Emphasis will be placed on the development of knowledge and preliminary skills to serve as an effective advocate for community and public health. Prerequisite: Advisory: Eligibility for ENGL-C1000 or ENGL-C1000E.

meta.prerequisites: Prerequisite: Advisory: Eligibility for ENGL-C1000 or ENGL-C1000E.

courses.title: Education for Healthful Living
courses.instructor: Mr. Nikki A. Ferguson, Mr. Miguel A. Johnson, Beth A. Goehring
courses.description: This course will require students to explore, analyze, personalize, and discuss the following issues as they relate to the essential components of health and wellness: nutrition, physical activity/exercise/fitness, weight control, eating disorders and body image, media influences, mental health, stress, violence, substance use/abuse, sexuality and sexual orientation, sexually transmitted infections, reproductive choices/contraception, relationships, disease prevention, environment, health care, aging, and general public health issues. Students will be taught the knowledge and skills necessary to implement lifestyle behaviors that can improve their health and well-being. Prerequisite: Eligibility for ENGL-C1000 or ENGL-C1000E.

meta.prerequisites: Prerequisite: Eligibility for ENGL-C1000 or ENGL-C1000E.

courses.title: Intro to Kinesiology
courses.instructor: Beth A. Goehring, Mr. Nikki A. Ferguson
courses.description: This course is intended to prepare students to enter the professional field of kinesiology. It is an introduction to the interdisciplinary approach to the study of human movement. An overview of the importance of the sub-disciplines in kinesiology will be discussed along with career opportunities in the areas of teaching, coaching, allied health, and fitness professions.

courses.title: First-Semester Mandarin
courses.description: This course introduces the fundamentals of Mandarin. The goals are to communicate in Mandarin through the development of skills in speaking, understanding, reading and writing, and to develop an appreciation of the Chinese culture.

courses.title: Reporting & Writing for Media
courses.description: This course presents the fundamentals of newswriting, newsgathering, and news judgment for newspapers and online news media. Emphasis is on accurate, clear and concise writing, organization and structure of news articles, language and style of journalism, reporting and newsgathering, revising and editing articles, and legal and ethical issues as they relate to journalistic reporting. Students will report and write based on their original interviews and research to produce news content. Experiences may include covering speeches, meetings and other events, writing under deadline and use of AP Style.
